description:
You are acting as a junior backup-site administrator who has just been asked to
    include a quick network-diagnostics step in the daily archive routine.

    Objectives
    ----------
    1. Create a new directory  
       /home/user/archive_logs  
       if it does not already exist.

    2. Run a **very small** network-diagnostic test (no more than two commands):
       • A 2-packet ICMP echo test (ping) to the loopback address 127.0.0.1.  
       • A traceroute that is limited to a maximum of 5 hops to the same address.

    3. Save the results to the single file  
       /home/user/archive_logs/backup_server_netcheck.log

    Required output format of /home/user/archive_logs/backup_server_netcheck.log
    ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
    The file must contain **exactly** the five labelled sections shown below
    (boxed for clarity; do NOT include the lines of asterisks):

    ************************************************************
    TIMESTAMP: <ISO-8601-date-and-time>
    PING_OUTPUT_START
    <every line of the ping command’s console output appears here verbatim>
    PING_OUTPUT_END
    TRACEROUTE_OUTPUT_START
    <every line of the traceroute command’s console output appears here verbatim>
    TRACEROUTE_OUTPUT_END
    ************************************************************

    • The first line must start with the literal word “TIMESTAMP: ” followed by the
      current date and time in the format YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS (24-hour clock).
    • The four marker words (PING_OUTPUT_START, PING_OUTPUT_END,
      TRACEROUTE_OUTPUT_START and TRACEROUTE_OUTPUT_END) must appear **each on
      their own line** and be written **exactly** as shown (all capitals, no extra
      spaces).
    • The diagnostic command outputs must be captured exactly as they appear in
      the terminal; do not trim, wrap, or otherwise alter them.
    • Place the ping output between PING_OUTPUT_START and PING_OUTPUT_END.
    • Place the traceroute output between TRACEROUTE_OUTPUT_START and
      TRACEROUTE_OUTPUT_END.

    What the grader will look for
    -----------------------------
    1. The file /home/user/archive_logs/backup_server_netcheck.log exists.
    2. The file contains the five required labelled sections in the correct order.
    3. The string “2 packets transmitted, 2 received” appears somewhere between
       PING_OUTPUT_START and PING_OUTPUT_END.
    4. The string “1  127.0.0.1” (hop 1 pointing at the loopback) appears somewhere
       between TRACEROUTE_OUTPUT_START and TRACEROUTE_OUTPUT_END.
    5. No additional text exists outside the required five labelled sections.

    You do not need root privileges to accomplish any of the steps.
    Use any commands or redirection techniques you like, but remember:
    • Only two diagnostic commands should be executed (one ping, one traceroute).
    • The log file must match the exact format given above.
truth:
Verification checklist for automated grader
    -------------------------------------------
    1. Path to check for existence:
       /home/user/archive_logs/backup_server_netcheck.log

    2. Mandatory literal lines or substrings the file MUST contain:
       a) The very first line must begin with “TIMESTAMP: ” followed by a
          19-character timestamp matching the regex:
          ^TIMESTAMP: [0-9]{4}-[0-9]{2}-[0-9]{2} [0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}$
       b) A line “PING_OUTPUT_START”
       c) A line containing both “2 packets transmitted, 2 received”
       d) A line “PING_OUTPUT_END”
       e) A line “TRACEROUTE_OUTPUT_START”
       f) At least one line that matches
          ^[ ]*1[ ]+127\.0\.0\.1
          (the first hop is the loopback)
       g) A line “TRACEROUTE_OUTPUT_END”

    3. The grader will also confirm that NO text appears before the “TIMESTAMP: ”
       line and that the markers occur in the exact prescribed order.

    4. Directory permissions:
       The agent will create /home/user/archive_logs/ with mode 755 (rwxr-xr-x)
       so it can write the log while still allowing read access to the grader.

    5. No network traffic other than to 127.0.0.1 will be generated, ensuring the
       test is self-contained and deterministic.
